text: The surgeon uses a combination of conventional, laparoscopic, and robotic ports, with robotic ports labeled R1 to R3. Dissection begins in a medial to lateral plane anterior to Todd's fascia, preserving the gonadal vessels and ureter. The inferior mesenteric artery and vein pedicle are sealed and divided. The dissection continues to the lateral limits, where adhesions with the colon are divided using a ligature device.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and divides the colon using a linear endogia stapler, ensuring precise separation of the tissue.

Robotic ports are placed under direct vision, and dissection is commenced in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane using robotic instruments. The robotic system offers enhanced dexterity and a magnified three-dimensional view of the operative field. The dissection progresses laterally down to the pelvic floor, with suction used to clear the smoke plume.
The surgeon continues to develop the dissection plane laterally, progressing towards the pelvic floor using robotic assistance.

The surgeon identifies and divides the round ligament of the uterus on the left side. The ureter is medialized, and dissection continues to expose the left uterine artery while ensuring the ureter remains medial.

The surgeon isolates the left uterine artery, applies diathermy, and divides it. The round ligament on the right is similarly isolated and divided, with the ureter medialized. The uterine artery is then sealed and divided.

The surgeon carefully develops the surgical plane between the bladder and uterus, continuing dissection down to the vault of the vagina. The anterior and lateral walls of the vagina are dissected out, while the posterior wall remains part of the en bloc specimen.
